title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Portage drafts zoning to block data centers from neighborhoods amid rising local resistance.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're looking at how one city is taking concrete steps to keep large facilities out of residential zones while communities across the state push back.
Graham, the lead story out of Portage really caught my eye. They're drafting new zoning language to block big data centers from most residential and agricultural areas.
That's right. The changes follow months of complaints about industrial projects creeping into rural edges of the city.
Why is this move significant for residents? What exactly are they trying to protect?
Kalamazoo area residents are worried about noise, increased traffic, and extra strain on the local electric grid if the rules get watered down.
So it's not just about keeping things quiet at night. The power demands could hit everyone's bills.
Exactly. These facilities use enormous amounts of electricity and water, and local officials are hearing that directly from neighbors.
How does this fit into the bigger picture we've seen lately? Are other places doing something similar?

Today alone we've got several townships hitting pause or passing strict new rules. Girard Township in Branch County approved tight setbacks, noise limits, and height caps that make large projects much harder.
That sounds like a strong signal. What do those rules actually allow?
Officials say smaller facilities can still go in if they meet the standards, but hyperscale campuses would face real hurdles near Coldwater.
And over in Eaton County, planners are flagging power and water risks from an Amazon-linked site.
Commissioners questioned the project in a study session and told staff to start drafting the county's first zoning rules for data centers.
Nearby residents are looking at higher utility bills and strained water supplies if it moves forward.
That's the common thread. People are asking what these projects deliver locally versus what they take.
Let's talk about Saline Township. They delayed a 12-year tax cut for an Oracle data center after residents raised concerns.
The board postponed action on the 50% abatement for the $43 billion project. Folks questioned the consent judgment and impacts on water, noise, and local costs.
That delay keeps the outcome uncertain for homeowners who could see rate hikes.

A rural township also hit pause on a major tax deal after dozens of residents showed up to object. The project would use huge amounts of power and water while delivering limited tax revenue for years.
How long has this kind of pushback been building?
The Portage changes come after months of complaints about industrial creep. Similar concerns have surfaced in multiple counties this week.
What happens next in Portage? Is this language likely to pass?
The draft aims to keep large facilities out of neighborhoods. Residents are watching closely to make sure it stays strong.
And in York Township they're scheduling a hearing on a data center moratorium.
Residents will weigh a temporary ban after similar pauses passed in nearby Adrian and other towns. They want time to study roads, water, and electricity rates.
That makes sense. People want breathing room before any big decisions.
In Ingham County, a proposal near Mason could bypass township review and go straight to state regulators.
Residents there fear losing local input on power use and tax deals.
The shift raises real questions about who gets the final say on these industrial projects.
Yesterday in Garfield Township, hundreds packed a hearing pushing for a moratorium.
Grand Traverse County planners recommended a temporary ban after residents protested power lines and industrial noise near homes and farms.
Locals even 30 minutes away are tracking it because any approved campus could affect regional electric rates.
And yesterday Michigan GOP candidates for governor split on whether they'd accept state or federal incentives for AI data centers.
Answers ranged from full support to calls for stricter local control. It's clearly becoming a campaign issue.
We don't have every candidate's full remarks yet, but the debate shows the topic is live in the primary.
Shifting to quick hits, another community is suing in federal court over nonstop data-center noise.
Residents claim an AI facility's constant hum has made normal life impossible. They're asking a judge to step in after local officials didn't act.
Homeowners within earshot could finally get relief or face years of disrupted sleep.
That's a tough spot for any neighborhood.
On deck tonight and tomorrow, listeners can actually attend these meetings.
Lenox Township Board meets tonight with data center zoning on the radar.
Tomorrow the Michigan Public Service Commission holds an open meeting overseeing large-load electric agreements.
And on July 13, Lansing City Council Development & Planning has a public hearing on a 182-day data center moratorium.
Those are the kinds of sessions where residents can speak directly.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.
Thanks for listening tonight.
Stay informed on these projects shaping Michigan's future.
Have a good evening.

episode_title: Portage Draws the Line as Michigan Townships Tighten Data Center Rules
